title: Bishop on the Long Diagonal
setup: Your king on **g6** locks the corner, your bishop on **a2** rakes the **a2-g8** diagonal, and the rook is one square from the kill.
hint: Capture the {{h7}} pawn — the bishop on **a2** prevents {{Kg8}}.
explanation:
## The Idea

A **rook + bishop corner mate**. The bishop's long diagonal denies the king the only escape square — {{g8}} — that the rook and king don't already control.

Play {{Rxh7#}}.

Why is the king stuck?

- The rook on h7 attacks h8 directly (file check)
- {{Kxh7}} — illegal, your king on **g6** defends h7
- {{g8}} — controlled by the bishop on **a2** (a2-g8 diagonal)
- {{g7}} — pawn-blocked? actually g7 still has a Black pawn — wait, even if it didn't, your king on **g6** would attack it
- Nothing can interpose between h7 and h8 (adjacent squares)

> **Takeaway:** A bishop on the long diagonal pointing into the enemy castled position is one of the most fearsome attackers in chess. Even a quiet bishop on a2 or b1 can be the silent partner of a corner mate.
